narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated a 48% chance that you would not pass—high enough to trigger rejection as a protective measure for both you and the program. This prediction stems from a close look at key elements of your profile, like your undergraduate GPA of 2.40, LSAT score of 25, Year 1 and Year 3 decile rankings both at 2, middle family income bracket, and your plan to attend full-time. Among students who passed the bar, the average undergraduate GPA is 3.24 and LSAT around 37, while top performers typically rank in the 6th decile or higher for those early years—your standings are notably lower, which weighed heavily here. The three most influential factors in pushing the model toward this failure prediction were your Year 3 decile ranking, your LSAT score, and your family income bracket, in that order of impact. Your Year 3 decile of 2 strongly nudged the prediction toward expecting a bar failure, as it signals performance below what the model sees in successful students. Similarly, your LSAT score of 25 pulled in the same direction, far under the typical benchmark for passers. On a more positive note, your middle family income actually worked in your favor, shifting the prediction slightly toward success compared to lower brackets, which are less common among bar passers. Being full-time is also a plus, aligning with over 90% of those who succeed. Overall, your profile landed you just on the riskier side compared to accepted students, whose stronger early academic deciles and test scores typically drop their failure odds well below 48%. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward 37 or higher, strengthening your undergraduate record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong letters highlighting your resilience and full-time commitment. Reapplying after addressing these core areas could shift your predicted odds dramatically in your favor—many students in similar positions have succeeded by targeting these exact improvements. Keep pushing; your determination as a full-time aspiring lawyer already sets a solid foundation.
